headline: Johor River Pollution Index Worsens for 5th Consecutive Year
context: DOE's 2024 Water Quality Index shows Sungai Johor deteriorated to Class III-IV across 62% of monitoring stations, the worst reading since monitoring began in 1998.

cards.big: Sungai Johor supplies water to 2.1 million people.
cards.sub: 62% of monitoring points now read Class III-IV — too polluted for conventional water treatment without costly upgrades.

cards.big: 487 factories discharge effluent into Sungai Johor's catchment.
cards.sub: DOE conducted 89 inspections in 2024. Only 14 compounds were issued — a 3% enforcement rate.

cards.big: Palm oil mill effluent accounts for 34% of organic pollution load.
cards.sub: Biological oxygen demand from 23 mills exceeds the river's natural absorption capacity by 260%.

cards.big: SAJ Water spent RM180M on additional treatment in 2024.
cards.sub: These costs are passed to consumers. Johor water tariffs rose 15% in 3 years — the fastest in Malaysia.

cards.big: Polluters save money. Consumers pay more for water. The river pays with its life.
River pollution is a subsidy from the public to polluters. Until discharge penalties exceed treatment savings, factories will rationally choose to pollute.
